message:
perf(btree): bounds-elide read_cell_pointer_inline hot path `BtCursor::read_cell_pointer_inline` is invoked once per cell during binary-search descent and once per visited child during full-tree walks, so it sits squarely on the cursor hot path. The pre-change shape: if ptr_offset + 2 > page.len() { return Err(FrankenError::DatabaseCorrupt { detail: format!(...), }); } Ok(u16::from_be_bytes([page[ptr_offset], page[ptr_offset + 1]])) had two tax items: 1. The in-body `format!` blocked LLVM from inlining the function. On the dyn-IO monomorphization (`BtCursor::read_cell_pointer_inline`) it showed up as its own ~0.37% MT8 self-time symbol in `tests/artifacts/perf/profiling-mt-mvcc-20260424T161631Z/perf_mt8_flat.txt`. 2. Even after the manual `ptr_offset + 2 > page.len()` guard, LLVM was emitting per-index bounds checks for `page[ptr_offset]` and `page[ptr_offset + 1]` on top of the manual guard. Same redundant-bounds-check pattern that commit 1f266968 fixed in `BtreePageHeader::parse` (10.7ns → 3.7ns). Fix in `crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs`: - Outline the corruption-error builder into a free `#[cold] #[inline(never)] fn cell_pointer_oob(...)`. One copy is shared across every `BtCursor

` monomorphization, and the heavy `format!` machinery is no longer in the function body that the inliner sees. - Replace the manual guard + indexed loads with one `page.get(ptr_offset..ptr_offset + 2).and_then(|s| s.try_into::<&[u8; 2]>().ok()).ok_or_else(|| cell_pointer_oob(...))`. The `try_into::<&[u8; 2]>` produces a single `[u8; 2]` reference with a single bounds check that LLVM's range-analysis can elide inside the descent loop, and the corruption path is a cold call to the outlined builder. - Add `#[inline]` to the function plus a doc-comment block citing the perf trace and the precedent commit so future readers know why the shape is shaped this way. Microbench in `crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cell.rs` (gated behind `#[ignore]`, run via `cargo test --release --ignored --nocapture bench_read_cell_pointer_inline`): - Mirror the cursor function shape — `read_cell_pointer_inline_- optimized` reproduces the new array-conversion + cold-outlined shape, `read_cell_pointer_inline_naive` reproduces the old manual-guard + format!-in-body shape. - Build a realistic interior-table page (header_size=12, page_no=7 so `header_offset_for_page` folds to 0, 32 cell pointers populated with deterministic offsets so the result sum is predictable but non-trivial). - Run 4M iterations × 5 trials per shape, sum results to defeat dead-code elimination, and assert both sums match each call. - Print per-trial `optimized=Xns naive=Yns delta=±Z%` so changes to the hot-path shape can be tracked over time. The bench is gated `#[ignore]` to keep it out of the default `cargo test` run. Co-Authored-By: Claude
